name: Fluoroquinolones and QTc prolongation
summary: Pooling the placebo-controlled thorough-QT data, single oral doses of moxifloxacin and levofloxacin both significantly prolong the Fridericia-corrected QT interval (QTcF) in healthy volunteers. Moxifloxacin pools to roughly 9–10 ms and levofloxacin to roughly 6–7 ms of placebo-corrected peak prolongation; ciprofloxacin has no comparable placebo-corrected data and shows minimal effect.
description: A small meta-analysis of placebo-corrected QTcF prolongation (∆∆QTcF) from thorough-QT (TQT) crossover studies in healthy adults. Each arm reports the peak (or C max) placebo-corrected change in QTcF after a single oral dose, with its confidence interval. Moxifloxacin is the standard positive control in TQT studies, so it is the most heavily characterized fluoroquinolone here.
interpretation: Both drugs prolong QTcF—the confidence interval excludes zero for each, so the pooled effect is flagged harmful. The effect is dose- and concentration-dependent: levofloxacin 1500 mg prolongs more than 1000 mg, and Täubel 2020's C max estimates (highest exposure) are the largest in each pool, which drives the heterogeneity. These are healthy-volunteer single doses; real-world risk depends on dose, accumulation, electrolytes, bradycardia, and other QT-prolonging drugs. A ~5–10 ms mean prolongation is a population signal, not an individual prediction of torsades.
methodologyNotes: Outcome is placebo-corrected ∆∆QTcF in milliseconds, entered per drug as a reported mean difference with its published CI and pooled live by random-effects (DerSimonian–Laird) on the linear scale—nothing is hardcoded. TQT studies report 90% CIs by convention; those arms are entered with ciLevel 90 so the standard error is recovered correctly, and every CI is then re-expressed at 95% so the forest plot shares one confidence level. Values were read directly from the source papers: Täubel 2010 (Table 1, peak placebo-corrected QTcF at 3.5 h)—moxifloxacin 400 mg 7.44 ms (95% CI 5.47, 9.42), levofloxacin 1000 mg 4.73 ms (95% CI 2.78, 6.99), levofloxacin 1500 mg 7.12 ms (95% CI 5.16, 9.09), all n=62; Chen 2015—moxifloxacin 400 mg peak QTcF 9.35 ms (90% CI 6.36, 12.34), n=24; Täubel 2020—concentration-effect ∆∆QTcF at overall C max: moxifloxacin 14.6 ms (90% CI 13.41, 15.77, n=133 pooled from two studies), levofloxacin 9.40 ms (90% CI 8.21, 10.59, n=62). Caveats: arms differ in correction method context, dose, and timepoint (peak time-matched vs C max model), and the three Täubel 2010 arms plus Täubel 2020 reuse the same crossover subjects, so within-outcome patient totals are not independent counts. Ciprofloxacin is deliberately absent: no thorough-QT study reports a placebo-corrected ∆∆QTcF with a CI for it (Noel 2003 reports only within-group change-from-baseline of ~2–5 ms with no placebo correction; ICU data show slight QTc shortening), which is itself consistent with ciprofloxacin's minimal QT liability. Single-arm, rate/incidence, QTcB-only, hERG/IC50, and paywalled reports from the wider request were not encodable as placebo-corrected ∆∆QTcF and were excluded.

outcomes.label: Moxifloxacin: QTcF prolongation (ms)
outcomes.description: Placebo-corrected peak/C max change in Fridericia-corrected QT (∆∆QTcF), milliseconds, after a single oral dose of moxifloxacin 400 mg. Positive values mean prolongation.

outcomes.label: Levofloxacin: QTcF prolongation (ms)
outcomes.description: Placebo-corrected peak/C max change in Fridericia-corrected QT (∆∆QTcF), milliseconds, after a single oral dose of levofloxacin. Positive values mean prolongation.

studies.author: Täubel
studies.citation: Taubel J, Naseem A, Harada T, Wang D, Arezina R, Lorch U. Levofloxacin can be used effectively as a positive control in thorough QT/QTc studies in healthy volunteers. Br J Clin Pharmacol. 2010;69(4):391-400.
studies.endpointDefinition: Largest time-matched placebo-corrected QTcF difference (3.5 h post-dose).
studies.notes: Crossover; same 62 subjects as the levofloxacin arms.

studies.author: Chen
studies.citation: Chen Q, Liu YM, Liu Y, Mendzelevski B, Chanter D, Pu HH, et al. Orally administered moxifloxacin prolongs QTc in healthy Chinese volunteers: a randomized, single-blind, crossover study. Acta Pharmacol Sin. 2015;36(4):448-456.

studies.author: Täubel
studies.citation: Täubel J, Prasad K, Rosano G, Ferber G, Wibberley H, Cole ST, et al. Effects of the fluoroquinolones moxifloxacin and levofloxacin on the QT subintervals: sex differences in ventricular repolarization. J Clin Pharmacol. 2020;60(3):400-408.
studies.population: Healthy adults, moxifloxacin data pooled from two TQT studies
studies.endpointDefinition: Concentration-effect model ∆∆QTcF at overall C max.

studies.author: Täubel
studies.citation: Taubel J, Naseem A, Harada T, Wang D, Arezina R, Lorch U. Levofloxacin can be used effectively as a positive control in thorough QT/QTc studies in healthy volunteers. Br J Clin Pharmacol. 2010;69(4):391-400.
studies.endpointDefinition: Largest time-matched placebo-corrected QTcF difference (3.5 h post-dose).
studies.notes: Crossover; same 62 subjects as the 1500 mg and moxifloxacin arms.
